title: Episode 2

jokes.text: I opened the cafe with my friend Boo. Yeah, she's dead now.
jokes.setup: Fleabag introduces her business partner warmly and casually, as if setting up a normal story about a friendship
jokes.punchline: The immediate pivot to 'Yeah, she's dead now' delivered with total flatness — the casual register makes the death land as a joke before the grief can register
jokes.explanation: Earns its 8+ by being a masterclass in register collision. The warmth of 'my friend Boo' followed immediately by 'Yeah, she's dead now' with zero emotional inflection establishes Fleabag's entire comedic and psychological voice in two sentences. It's only four words for the punchline but they carry the whole show's thesis — grief processed as dark comedy. Character-specific: only Fleabag talks about her dead best friend this way. The 'Yeah' is doing enormous work, adding practiced casualness as if she's said this before.

jokes.text: My sister: I have two degrees, a husband and a Burberry coat.
jokes.setup: We are introduced to Fleabag's sister, who we expect to say something warm or familial
jokes.punchline: Her self-introduction is a status inventory: degrees, husband, designer coat — delivered as if these constitute a personality
jokes.explanation: A character introduction that doubles as a punchline. The Burberry coat as the climax of the list — placed after 'degrees' and 'husband' on equal footing — is the tell. It's not just that she's status-obsessed; the coat being mentioned as an achievement on par with higher education is the insight. Economy is exceptional: seven words establish the character completely.

jokes.text: Dad's way of coping with two motherless daughters was to buy us tickets to feminist lectures, start fucking our godmother and eventually stop calling.
jokes.setup: Fleabag frames this as an explanation of Dad's coping strategy, suggesting something thoughtful or paternal
jokes.punchline: The escalating trio of responses — feminist lectures (absurdly well-meaning), fucking the godmother (catastrophic betrayal), and eventually stop calling (devastating abandonment) — each step is worse than the last, delivered as equivalent items on a list
jokes.explanation: Justifies 9+ confidently. The construction is near-perfect: three items, each worse than the last, with the first item (feminist lectures) being so specifically absurd in its well-intentioned uselessness that it earns immediate laughter before the real darkness hits. 'Start fucking our godmother' is the explosive mid-escalation. 'Eventually stop calling' lands as the true gut-punch because it's the most mundane of the three and the most emotionally ruinous. The list format makes three tragedies feel like a comic rhythm. 'Motherless daughters' in the setup does heavy lifting — it's not just a broken family, it's grief. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Please don't contact me or turn up at my house drunk in your underwear. It won't work this time. / It will.
jokes.setup: Claire issues what sounds like a firm, desperate boundary — 'it won't work this time' implies this has happened before
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's single-word response 'It will' delivered with complete confidence, acknowledging the manipulation and the pattern simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through economy and character integration. The comedy lives entirely in those two words — 'It will' — which simultaneously reveal Fleabag's self-awareness about her own manipulation, her confidence in its effectiveness, and the sad codependency of the sibling relationship. No wasted syllables. Claire's framing ('drunk in your underwear') also does retroactive world-building work: this has happened before, multiple times.

jokes.text: I think my period's coming.
jokes.setup: Music has been playing over a montage — emotional, charged setup. Fleabag breaks the mood with a direct-to-camera address
jokes.punchline: The total mundaneness of 'I think my period's coming' as the thing she chooses to share after the emotional music cue — bathetic deflation of whatever drama was building
jokes.explanation: A textbook Fleabag move — using bodily reality to cut through emotional inflation. The bathetic pivot from AWOLNATION scoring her life to a period cramp is both funny and characterizing. Works better in context (with the music) than it reads on paper.

jokes.text: Nice haircut. / It's better.
jokes.setup: Harry answers the door clearly distressed. Fleabag's opener to her ex is 'Nice haircut' — an aggressively casual social nicety in an obviously fraught situation
jokes.punchline: 'It's better' — Harry accepts the compliment in context, but the word 'better' implies the previous haircut was bad enough to note, which he just... goes with
jokes.explanation: Small, effective deflection comedy. Fleabag's use of small talk to avoid emotional confrontation is a recurring character note. 'Better' rather than 'good' is the small grace note that makes it funny rather than just awkward.

jokes.text: Give me two. [Beat] Gang bangs, Asian, I'd put a tenner on it.
jokes.setup: Martin says 'give me two' — needing two minutes in his study. Fleabag addresses the camera with mock-casual certainty about what he's watching
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'gang bangs, Asian' — not just 'porn' but a confident, specific prediction delivered as if she's done this mental calculation before
jokes.explanation: Martin is established immediately as a creep and Fleabag as someone who has already made peace with this fact about him. The specificity of the sub-genres is what makes it funny rather than just the baseline 'he's watching porn' observation. Gets a rewatch bonus because it pays off massively at 23:40.

jokes.text: Oh, well, it must be my lucky day. / You said she only likes to talk to people she fancies.
jokes.setup: Martin says it must be his lucky day when told Fleabag wants to talk to him — implying he thinks she fancies him
jokes.punchline: Claire's flat confirmation that this is indeed the logic being used — which is both an insult to Martin (she doesn't believe it) and a weird insight into Fleabag's social behaviour
jokes.explanation: Works as character comedy primarily. Martin's creepiness is already established; Claire's dry delivery of his own logic back at him shows her contempt for him without any overt hostility. Medium-tier.

jokes.text: Martin has been sent to his study; Fleabag looks at camera after Claire says 'He's organising your surprise birthday party.'
jokes.setup: Claire needs Martin gone; she uses the cover story that he's organising a surprise birthday party for Fleabag
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's look to camera — understanding that Claire has just invented a lie that now obligates Fleabag to perform surprise, plus the comic absurdity of Martin organising anything
jokes.explanation: The fourth-wall look is doing double work: letting us in on the lie and signaling that Fleabag already knows she'll have to deal with the consequences. Rewatch bonus because the party then occupies the rest of the episode and Fleabag does indeed end up organising it herself.

jokes.text: I have a week to organise that now. / Best of luck.
jokes.setup: Fleabag realises the cover story has now trapped her in party planning duty
jokes.punchline: Claire's 'Best of luck' — delivered with zero sympathy, as if she's entirely unaware or unconcerned that she just created this problem
jokes.explanation: Claire's cheerful abdication of responsibility is a solid character beat. The comedy is in her complete absence of guilt. Functional, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Stole it from a market. / Market artist.
jokes.setup: Martin asks where the sculpture came from and who the artist is — standard art dealer due diligence
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's casual confession that she stole it, then 'just a... market artist' — the pause before 'market artist' suggests she's improvising a cover for 'I stole this and don't know'
jokes.explanation: The confession is funny because it's so matter-of-fact. The ellipsis before 'market artist' reveals she's just now constructing a plausible story. Competent character comedy.

jokes.text: Or I'll tell her you were watching gang bangs.
jokes.setup: Martin has just said 'You've got nothing on me, princess' — daring Fleabag to threaten him
jokes.punchline: Fleabag deploys exactly the information she pre-loaded at 02:12 — she does have something on him, and it's precisely what she predicted
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ as a perfectly executed callback. The joke at 02:12 was funny as an observation; this confirms it wasn't just a guess but intelligence she actually has on him. The structure is satisfying: Martin's confident 'You've got nothing on me' is immediately demolished by the very thing she predicted. The callback payoff makes both jokes retroactively funnier. Economy is perfect — just the one specific detail, not a list.

jokes.text: Please don't do that again. [pause] I wasn't, by the way.
jokes.setup: Martin has been exposed and threatened. His initial response sounds like a concession — 'please don't do that again'
jokes.punchline: The pause, then 'I wasn't, by the way' — he absolutely was, and we know it, and the pause before the denial makes the lie obvious and pathetic
jokes.explanation: The timing of this is everything — it's a two-beat joke. The pause is where the laugh lives, because the pause is the moment he decides denial is his best play. 'I wasn't, by the way' is a beautiful specimen of the unconvincing protestation — it's the specific tag 'by the way' that makes it funny rather than just 'I wasn't.' The 'by the way' implies casualness and added information, which is the exact wrong register for a denial.

jokes.text: You're not like other girls. You can... keep up.
jokes.setup: Dad says Harry 'used to make you laugh,' trying to find something positive. Fleabag gives an example of a Harry compliment
jokes.punchline: 'You're not like other girls. You can... keep up.' — the pause before 'keep up' reveals this as a 'compliment' that's actually deeply condescending, and Fleabag delivers it with full ironic awareness of how bad it is
jokes.explanation: The ellipsis before 'keep up' is crucial — it implies the original compliment built to something impressive, then delivers 'keep up' as the entire content of Harry's admiration. Skewers a very specific type of male condescension (the 'cool girl' compliment) with economy. Waller-Bridge's awareness of what she's satirising is sharp here.

jokes.text: I admire how much Harry commits to our break-ups.
jokes.setup: We've seen Harry dramatically packing up his things again. Fleabag addresses camera with apparent sincerity
jokes.punchline: Admiring his commitment to break-ups rather than to the relationship — the inversion of where one typically wants a partner's commitment
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through economy and structural elegance. The word 'commits' is perfectly chosen — it's the relationship word, the virtue you want your partner to have, deployed in exactly the wrong context. 'Our break-ups' (plural, possessive) treats the break-ups as a shared ongoing project, which is both funny and sad. Highly quotable, applicable beyond the show.

jokes.text: A few times, he's even cleaned the whole flat. Like it's a crime scene.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is elaborating on Harry's break-up commitment — she mentioned he 'goes the extra mile'
jokes.punchline: 'Like it's a crime scene' — the flat as crime scene implies Fleabag as the crime, or the relationship as something so catastrophic it requires forensic cleaning
jokes.explanation: The crime scene simile is unexpected and resonant — it has a dark undercurrent given the show's themes of guilt and Boo's death that the viewer may not fully process on first watch. Strong economy.

jokes.text: I've considered timing a break-up for when the flat needs a bit of a going over.
jokes.setup: The previous joke established Harry cleans during break-ups. This is the logical-but-monstrous extension of that observation
jokes.punchline: Fleabag has considered using the emotional devastation of a break-up as a cleaning scheduling hack — the domesticity of 'a bit of a going over' making the emotional cynicism even more brutal
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional escalation joke. The setup (he cleans during break-ups) is already funny; arriving at the conclusion that this could be used instrumentally is both perfectly logical and completely depraved. The register collision — the phrase 'a bit of a going over' is pure mundane British domestic talk — alongside the cold relational calculus makes this the kind of line that makes a room gasp before laughing. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: I don't think this is working. [Harry appears to be attempting to fold/pack dramatically and it's not going well]
jokes.setup: Harry is in the middle of a dramatic, committed break-up packing sequence
jokes.punchline: His acknowledgment mid-task that whatever he's doing physically isn't working — the commitment to the dramatic gesture undermined by practical incompetence
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy inferred from transcript context. The line 'I don't think this is working' is ambiguous — could refer to the relationship, the packing, or some physical task — and the ambiguity is probably intentional. Moderate impact.

jokes.text: But he always leaves... him to come back for.
jokes.setup: Fleabag seems about to make a cynical observation about Harry's departures. The pause before 'him to come back for' suggests a visual reveal
jokes.punchline: Presumably Harry has left behind something — likely a cherished object — that functions as his guaranteed excuse to return. The object's nature is the visual joke
jokes.explanation: Partially a visual gag that's difficult to fully assess from transcript alone. The joke is in what he's left behind — the setup is stronger than the punchline as transcribed.

jokes.text: I'm not obsessed with sex, I just can't stop thinking about it. The performance of it. The awkwardness of it. The drama of it. The moment you realise someone wants your body. Not so much the feeling of it.
jokes.setup: Fleabag insists she's not obsessed with sex — direct denial that functions as a setup
jokes.punchline: The list of aspects she does think about is extensive and specific; 'Not so much the feeling of it' is the dark revelation at the end — she's interested in sex as a form of performance and validation, not pleasure
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through character integration and the dark final turn. The list of 'the performance of it / the awkwardness of it / the drama of it' builds a rhythm that makes 'not so much the feeling of it' hit as a genuine psychological revelation. It's funny but also heartbreaking — she uses sex as something other than physical pleasure, which maps onto her grief and dissociation. The joke is the character. 'I'm not obsessed with sex, I just can't stop thinking about it' is also a perfect micro-joke in itself: the distinction is meaningless and Fleabag knows it.

jokes.text: I took half an hour trying to look nice and I ended up looking amazing. Just one of those days.
jokes.setup: Fleabag frames this as a relatable oversharing moment — the 'trying to look nice' setup implies moderate success at best
jokes.punchline: The escalation to 'amazing' and 'just one of those days' — delivered without irony, pure vanity stated as fact
jokes.explanation: 'Just one of those days' is the key — it's the phrase you use for bad luck or minor disasters, repurposed here for unexpectedly looking amazing. The casual narcissism is funny because it's delivered without any self-consciousness.

jokes.text: Yeah, you check me out, chub-chub, because it's never gonna happen.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is apparently being ogled by someone on the street while heading to the cafe.
jokes.punchline: She's internally narrating the encounter with total confidence, calling the man 'chub-chub' and dismissing him — the joke is her absolute self-assurance while also being slightly mean about a stranger.
jokes.explanation: Entertaining character voice — her internal monologue is funny in the way it reveals both her confidence and her cruelty. 'Chub-chub' is a nicely specific pejorative. Part of a longer street-harassment riff that builds to a better punchline.

jokes.text: Oh, God, he can't believe how attractive I am. Kind of worried I'm going to make a sex offender out of the poor guy.
jokes.setup: A man on the street is looking at Fleabag. She's narrating her internal experience of being attractive
jokes.punchline: The pivot from vanity to 'worried I'm going to make a sex offender out of the poor guy' — her attractiveness framed as a public safety hazard
jokes.explanation: The self-aggrandizing internal monologue punctured by the sex offender line — which simultaneously skewers female socialization around male attention (it must be her fault somehow) and Fleabag's particular brand of chaotic self-regard. The sympathy for 'the poor guy' is a nice layer.

jokes.text: Here we go. This better be good. Here we go. [Man coughs and says 'Hooker slag.']
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been building up to what the man is going to say — 'this better be good' implies she expects a compliment or chat-up line
jokes.punchline: 'Hooker slag' — the anti-climax of her expectations and the sudden violence of the insult. Fleabag's hopeful 'here we go' makes the casual misogyny of what follows especially bleak and funny
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through superb misdirection structure. Fleabag has spent thirty seconds building up the anticipation of what this man will say — 'this better be good' is an invitation to the audience to share her expectation. The cough before the punchline is even a mini-setup. Then 'Hooker slag' — two words, maximum violence, maximum deflation of everything she's constructed. The joke's darkness is part of its power: it's simultaneously a gag about street harassment, about Fleabag's deluded self-regard, and about the gap between how women imagine they're being perceived and the reality.

jokes.text: Oh. Dropped my cucumber.
jokes.setup: A man has apparently entered the cafe and there's been some kind of reaction; Fleabag addresses the camera
jokes.punchline: The cucumber drop — presumably phallic, presumably embarrassing, delivered with maximum understatement
jokes.explanation: Low-key phallic visual gag. The understatement sells it. Not particularly sophisticated but gets a smile.

jokes.text: That'll be, er, £12.55, please. / London, eh?
jokes.setup: Customer orders a cheese sandwich
jokes.punchline: £12.55 for a cheese sandwich — the price is the joke. The customer's response 'London, eh?' is the resigned acceptance that functions as the secondary punchline
jokes.explanation: Solid observational comedy about London prices, but it's a well-worn joke. 'London, eh?' as resigned acceptance is funny in its brevity and accuracy. Character integration is weak — any London cafe owner could say this. Gets by on economy.

jokes.text: Where do I recognise her? Is she famous? / Boo's death hit the papers. Local cafe girl gets hit by bike and a car and another bike.
jokes.setup: Customer asks if Fleabag is famous. The answer turns out to be grimly specific
jokes.punchline: The triple escalation of Boo's death — bike, car, another bike — the final 'another bike' being the grotesque topper that makes the already-tragic death absurd
jokes.explanation: The callback to Boo's death is executed with the same deadpan register as the original mention, but now we get the specifics. 'Local cafe girl gets hit by bike and a car and another bike' — the rhythm here is crucial. Each vehicle adds to the mounting absurdity. 'And another bike' is the perfect topper: the first bike was terrible, the car was catastrophic, another bike arriving to finish the job is both ludicrous and somehow makes it worse. The newspaper headline framing ('local cafe girl') reduces Boo to a tabloid story, which adds a layer of dark comedy about how grief gets processed publicly.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, I don't have any change. [Customer has presumably not paid enough]
jokes.setup: After the heavy emotional revelation about Boo, the customer interaction continues mundanely
jokes.punchline: The transaction just... continues. Fleabag's cafe is struggling financially, and this small detail (no change available) suggests a financially precarious operation
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the bathos — moving straight from grief about Boo to 'I don't have change.' The comedy of financial precarity. Modest impact.

jokes.text: The next man who walks in here is getting ridden to death.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is alone, having not had the sexual encounter she wanted. She makes a direct-to-camera vow
jokes.punchline: 'Ridden to death' — an extreme, vaguely medieval-sounding form of sex-as-aggression, delivered with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: Then Dad immediately walks in — the punchline is the universe's response to Fleabag's declaration. 'Ridden to death' as a phrase is funny on its own (archaic, extreme, oddly equestrian), but its comedy is doubled by what immediately follows. Character-specific: only Fleabag would use sex as a coping mechanism stated this baldly.

jokes.text: Dad! / Not ideal.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just declared the next man through the door will be 'ridden to death'
jokes.punchline: Dad walks in. Fleabag's 'Not ideal' is the perfect understatement for the specific horror of this timing
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ comfortably. Perfect setup/punchline structure: the 'ridden to death' vow creates the setup, Dad's entrance is the universe's joke, and 'Not ideal' is Fleabag's two-word acknowledgment of the cosmic comedy. 'Not ideal' is the ideal response — it's British understatement applied to an incest-adjacent horror scenario, and the gap between the event and the descriptor is where all the laughter lives. The economy is maximal: two words for a perfect punchline. The structure is textbook.

jokes.text: Dad's extended attempt to start a meaningful conversation repeatedly failing — ellipses, false starts, 'er...' — before pivoting to 'Did you take the sculpture?'
jokes.setup: Dad has come to have an emotional conversation with Fleabag, presumably about their estrangement
jokes.punchline: His inability to form a sentence about feelings, culminating in the pivot to the sculpture — which is a concrete, accusatory question — reveals he came for the sculpture, not the emotional reckoning
jokes.explanation: The cringe comedy of the emotionally inarticulate parent is well-observed. The revelation that he came about the sculpture (which Fleabag took) rather than genuine emotional reconnection is a quiet character tragedy played as comedy. The stuttering 'er...' rhythm is funny as sustained performance.

jokes.text: No. / What sculpture? / Oh, right, fine, good. Good, you said no and... ..that means I can go.
jokes.setup: Dad asks directly if Fleabag took the sculpture. She says no. He appears to accept this completely despite having no reason to believe her
jokes.punchline: 'That means I can go' — Dad's only reason for visiting was to confirm the sculpture was safe; the emotional visit was a pretext, and he's relieved to have an exit
jokes.explanation: 'That means I can go' is the perfect encapsulation of Dad's emotional avoidance — he's set up a conditional visit where one answer sets him free. The comedy is in how transparent his relief is and how aware both characters are of what's really happening.

jokes.text: Oh, right, fine, good. Good, you said no and... ..that means I can go.
jokes.setup: Dad has received a transparently false denial about the sculpture.
jokes.punchline: He accepts it with enormous relief — 'that means I can go' — the emotional conversation was also just about giving himself permission to leave.
jokes.explanation: 'That means I can go' is a devastating character revelation dressed as comedy relief. Dad came ostensibly to connect with his daughter — but what he really wanted was an excuse to leave. 'Good, you said no and that means I can go' is the saddest, funniest summation of avoidant parenting. It could only be this character.

jokes.text: Hey. Do you do, like, hot organic-y food? / Of course. / Like, risotto. / Yeah. Sure. Grab a seat.
jokes.setup: A customer asks if they do 'hot organic-y food' — a non-specific millennial food request delivered with upspeak
jokes.punchline: Fleabag confidently says 'of course' to the extremely vague 'organic-y' and then immediately confirms they can do risotto — a fairly specific dish, now apparently on the menu
jokes.explanation: Mild observational comedy about the financially desperate cafe owner saying yes to anything. 'Organic-y' as a non-word is the small joke. Fleabag's 'of course' to a meal she's probably now going to improvise is the character beat.

jokes.text: Fucked me up the arse. / What are you getting?
jokes.setup: Fleabag is apparently at a shop, greeting someone she knows. She identifies him to camera (or internally) with complete flatness
jokes.punchline: 'What are you getting?' delivered immediately after the identification — the social niceties continue as normal despite the information we've just been given
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through economy and structural perfection. The joke is entirely in the juxtaposition: intimate identifying information delivered in the register of a shopping receipt, immediately followed by completely normal social interaction. The man gets no identifier beyond what he did; 'What are you getting?' as the immediate follow-up treats the information as simply his name-tag. Highly Fleabag-specific — only she would introduce someone this way to camera.

jokes.text: Oh, just these. For my... ..tiny, bleeding vagina. / Hot.
jokes.setup: He asks what she's getting. She holds up what are presumably tampons/sanitary products, then names them with clinical specificity
jokes.punchline: His response of 'Hot' — treating 'tiny, bleeding vagina' as a come-on rather than bodily information; his response makes the overshare retroactively sexy, which is absurd
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through the one-word punchline doing all the work. 'Tiny, bleeding vagina' is already funny — the clinical specificity of 'tiny' alongside 'bleeding' delivered as an identifier rather than a complaint. But 'Hot' as his response transforms it into a different kind of joke: he's either in on the absurdity or genuinely finds period products arousing, and neither explanation is less funny. The call-and-response structure is exceptionally tight.

jokes.text: Stock cubes. / Hot.
jokes.setup: She asks what he's getting. The expected response is something similarly unexpected or funny
jokes.punchline: 'Stock cubes' — profoundly mundane. Fleabag's mirrored 'Hot' turns the gag into a callback to his 'Hot' thirty seconds ago, now shown to be a meaningless reflex rather than a compliment
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through the echo structure. 'Stock cubes' is the perfect anti-climax to the earlier intimacy. Fleabag's returning 'Hot' to describe stock cubes is both a mirror joke and a small character moment — she's matching his energy, which either means she fancies him or she's taking the piss. The maximum economy of the exchange (four words total) generating a double laugh is impressive craft.

jokes.text: I hope it's a light flow. / Oh, it never is. / It never... ..is.
jokes.setup: Continuation of their intimate but casual exchange about her period
jokes.punchline: The repeated 'It never is' — the pause before the second '...is' implies she's either enjoying the conversation or giving him time to absorb the information about her heavy flow as a romantic revelation
jokes.explanation: The extended beat on 'It never... ..is' — the pause treating her heavy periods as a solemn romantic truth being shared — is an understated gem. The conversation has the rhythm of flirtation but the content of a GP consultation.

jokes.text: Yes! / Yes, fucking yes, please, yes. / Yes. / Cool.
jokes.setup: He asks if she's free later. She says yes — but the transcript shows her internal and external responses
jokes.punchline: Her internal 'Yes, fucking yes, please, yes' against the outward 'Yes. / Cool' — the gap between volcanic enthusiasm and studied casual delivery
jokes.explanation: The mockumentary/direct-address format allows Fleabag to show us the internal versus the external simultaneously. The contrast between 'Yes, fucking yes, please, yes' and 'Cool' is a comic gap that only works because we're in her confidence. Character-specific delivery.

jokes.text: Yes! I'm so happy with my body now. I don't have to define myself by how I look because I've just got a fucking great body. Yeah, I can do other stuff now!
jokes.setup: A woman is declaring body positivity and liberation from physical self-obsession
jokes.punchline: The liberation from body-obsession is expressed entirely through confidence in her 'fucking great body' — she's replaced insecurity with narcissism and called it progress
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical observation about body positivity discourse that tips into self-congratulation. 'Yeah, I can do other stuff now!' as the payoff is very specific — the achievement unlocked framing applied to self-acceptance is funny and pointed.

jokes.text: Mike wants to start trying for a baby. / OK. / No... I can't blow this body on a baby, Steph. I'm going to have to leave him.
jokes.setup: Seemingly warm intimate revelation about her partner wanting a baby
jokes.punchline: Her response is that she can't 'blow this body on a baby' and she'll have to leave him — the body she just achieved through 'not defining herself by her looks' is now being protected from reproduction at the cost of her relationship
jokes.explanation: The callback structure makes this much stronger — the body positivity declaration immediately preceding this reveals the complete logic: she's not liberated from body-obsession, she's more enslaved by it. 'I can't blow this body on a baby' is an extraordinarily quotable line. 'I'm going to have to leave him' as the inevitable conclusion, delivered flatly, is the topper.

jokes.text: Oh, shit. Oh. That is not hygienic.
jokes.setup: The women in the gym/café are apparently eating something directly from a container or surface — Fleabag witnesses this
jokes.punchline: Her prim 'that is not hygienic' as a reaction to whatever chaos is happening around her
jokes.explanation: Modest visual gag. Without the visual component this joke is relatively weak on paper. The comedy is presumably in what we see.

jokes.text: I suppose you should meet Hillary. [pause] The most important thing is, if you don't like it, we can't take it back, OK?
jokes.setup: The phrase 'meet Hillary' suggests a person; 'if you don't like it we can't take it back' sounds like an adoption or a pet — but specifically the warning about returning it
jokes.punchline: It's a guinea pig. The gift-giving logic and the 'as long as I can wear it or eat it' response escalates the absurdity
jokes.explanation: The build to the guinea pig reveal is nicely structured. 'As long as I can wear it or eat it I'm happy' as Fleabag's birthday wish criterion is an unexpectedly medieval set of standards that pays off with the guinea pig.

jokes.text: As long as I can wear it or eat it I'm happy. / You can do both of those things.
jokes.setup: Fleabag sets her gift criteria at 'wearable or edible'
jokes.punchline: Boo's 'You can do both of those things' — confirming that a guinea pig technically meets both criteria, which is horrifying and funny
jokes.explanation: Boo's cheerful 'you can do both of those things' is the darker joke here — she's aware of the implication and is comfortable with it. The economy is excellent: one sentence, maximum dark comedy.

jokes.text: You idiot! / Escape artist.
jokes.setup: The guinea pig has apparently immediately escaped or caused chaos upon being revealed
jokes.punchline: 'Escape artist' — Boo knows the guinea pig's character well enough to have a pre-prepared response, suggesting this is a known personality trait
jokes.explanation: Small, sweet joke — the guinea pig has a character trait the giver knows about. 'Escape artist' as a known fact about a gift you're giving someone is an odd choice that's funny and also characterizes Boo as someone who would think an escape-artist guinea pig is an ideal gift.

jokes.text: I don't feel anything about guinea pigs, they're pointless, but Boo took Hillary very seriously as a gift and, soon, everything became guinea pig-related.
jokes.setup: Fleabag narrates her genuine indifference to guinea pigs
jokes.punchline: The contrast between Fleabag's 'pointless' and Boo's absolute commitment to guinea pig culture — 'everything became guinea pig-related'
jokes.explanation: 'They're pointless' as a genuine assessment of guinea pigs, delivered with Fleabag's characteristic flatness, contrasted with Boo's absolute commitment, characterizes both women economically. Gets a rewatch bonus because it also makes Fleabag keeping Hillary feel like an act of grief.

jokes.text: So reliable. Utterly inaccessible. Relentlessly profound. All he wants is to get you in the bath and ask questions like... 'What are you afraid of?' And you find yourself saying things like... 'I guess... losing the currency of youth.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is describing a man she's sleeping with — the description is of a certain archetypal 'intellectual deep man'
jokes.punchline: The series of high-minded phrases — 'relentlessly profound' — culminate in her saying 'losing the currency of youth' as if it's something she'd never say under normal circumstances. The bath questions have made her talk like a creative writing student
jokes.explanation: Targets a very specific type — the Meaningful Man who makes you talk about your fears in the bath — with accuracy. 'Losing the currency of youth' as something you'd only say if a man in a bath asked you what you're afraid of is spot-on. 'Relentlessly profound' is a nice phrase that works as mild satire.

jokes.text: Ask me a question. / When did you realise you were so good looking? / I knew I was different when I was about nine, but shit got real around 11. Shit got real? / You know... Aunts got weird.
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks him to ask her something meaningful. He asks about when she realised she was good-looking — which is ostensibly a compliment but also a narcissism trap
jokes.punchline: 'I knew I was different when I was about nine, but shit got real around 11. Aunts got weird.' — the phrase 'shit got real around 11' applied to beauty is very funny, and 'Aunts got weird' as the specific evidence is perfect
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ with confidence. 'Shit got real around 11' applied to beauty realisation is unexpected and funny on its own — the AAVE-inflected phrase applied to adolescent self-discovery in a middle-class English context. But 'Aunts got weird' as the supporting evidence is what makes this exceptional. It's specific, accurate (aunts do get weird around pubescent girls), and leaves enormous room for the audience's imagination. The callback to Fleabag's established narcissism (from joke 22) makes it richer. This line has genuine quotability — 'shit got real, aunts got weird' — highly shareable.

jokes.text: Do you ever feel lonely? / Yeah. Of course. Do you? / Never.
jokes.setup: A meaningful-man bath question: 'Do you ever feel lonely?' Fleabag answers with vulnerable honesty
jokes.punchline: Her return question is met with 'Never' — total refusal of emotional reciprocity from a man who instigated the emotional conversation
jokes.explanation: The comedy and the pathos are inseparable here. He asked her about loneliness to get her to open up, then refuses the same question entirely. 'Never' with no elaboration is both funny (in its bluntness after his 'relentlessly profound' setup) and revealing about his emotional unavailability. The joke earns extra credit for what it implies about why Fleabag is sleeping with this specific man.

jokes.text: Do you want some pineapple? / Yeah. [then sex sounds immediately follow]
jokes.setup: After a charged emotional exchange, he offers pineapple
jokes.punchline: She says yes, and then apparently they have sex — 'pineapple' was the transition into sex, or the pineapple question was asked in the context of already-beginning sex
jokes.explanation: The bathos of pineapple as the transition into sex is a nice small joke. The mundaneness of the fruit makes it funny; the speed of the transition (zero to sex from 'pineapple') amplifies it.

jokes.text: God, yeah! Oh, they're so small! They are so small! ... God, they are so fucking tiny! Yeah, I guess they're... Oh, God, they're hardly even there! I mean, what the fuck even are they? / Bit much. / Excuse me!
jokes.setup: They are apparently having sex; Fleabag appears to be enthusiastically commenting on something anatomical about him
jokes.punchline: The escalating hyperbole — 'they're so small... hardly even there... what the fuck even are they?' — whatever she's describing is apparently remarkably small. His wounded 'Bit much' and her indignant 'Excuse me!' as the exchange ends is the cap
jokes.explanation: The sustained escalation of commentary — going from enthusiastic to potentially devastating — with the ambiguity about what she's commenting on. His 'Bit much' is perfectly understated for the injury implied. Her 'Excuse me!' in offense at his 'bit much' is the topper: she's offended that he's offended.

jokes.text: Bit much. / Excuse me!
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just delivered an increasingly alarmed commentary on the man's anatomy.
jokes.punchline: 'Bit much. Excuse me!' — the wounded, clipped British response to being told your genitals are confusing. 'Bit much' is the understatement; 'Excuse me!' is the indignation.
jokes.explanation: The two-word escalation 'Bit much. Excuse me!' is excellent — 'bit much' is his generous concession, 'Excuse me!' is the point where he's decided she's crossed a line. The pause between them is comedically significant. His wounded dignity is funnier than the preceding tirade because it's so controlled.

jokes.text: I'm having a Harry panic. Madame Ovary's telling me to run back to safe place, I can make baby in safe place, but you've got to ride it out. Mustn't call Ha—
jokes.setup: Post-sex, Fleabag is experiencing a familiar anxiety about reverting to Harry
jokes.punchline: 'Madame Ovary' as a name for her reproductive instincts — the pun on Madame Bovary, characterizing her biological drive as a classic literary figure prone to disastrous romantic choices
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through the quality of the pun and its character integration. 'Madame Ovary' is a genuinely excellent piece of wordplay — it's a pun on Madame Bovary, a novel about a woman who makes catastrophically bad romantic decisions out of romantic idealism, applied to Fleabag's biological clock/safe-relationship instinct. The literary reference is earned rather than name-droppy because it's accurate. High quotability — this phrase could travel independently of the show.

jokes.text: Thanks for coming. / Were you busy? / No, I was in the interval... of Cats. The musical.
jokes.setup: Harry has come when called. She asks if he was busy. His answer reveals what he was doing
jokes.punchline: 'Of Cats. The musical.' — the clarification that Cats is a musical (unnecessary, pedantic) attached to the answer is what makes this funny. He was watching Cats, he left at interval to come to her, and he felt he needed to specify it's a musical
jokes.explanation: 'The musical' as a clarifying tag is perfect — who else is seeing Cats? Nobody thinks they're seeing the dramatic monologue. It's the nervous social habit of over-specifying, which is a Harry character note. Also: he was at Cats with a work friend, which positions him as someone whose life is fine without her, which makes the 'I dropped everything to come' worse somehow.

jokes.text: Was it good? / Really good, actually. Really good. / Sorry for interrupting. / No, that's OK. I, er... I got the feeling it wasn't going to end well for the cats, so probably good to remember them like that before they all...
jokes.setup: Harry assures her Cats was really good, she apologises for interrupting it, and he begins to justify why it was OK to leave
jokes.punchline: His trailing off — 'before they all...' — implying the cats die at the end of Cats (they don't, famously — they go to cat heaven which is arguably worse), delivered as if he's worried about spoilers for a well-known 1981 musical
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: the cats don't die (they ascend to the Heaviside Layer, which is its own kind of comedy), Harry is covering for why he left, and his trailing off with a spoiler concern for a decades-old musical is specifically funny. The 'Really good, actually. Really good.' repetition before this also does work — the 'actually' shows he was surprised to enjoy it, which makes his abandonment of it funnier.

jokes.text: I wish he'd just fuck me. All he wants to do is make love.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is with Harry and things are becoming intimate. She directly addresses camera with a complaint
jokes.punchline: The inversion of the expected complaint — she doesn't want intimacy, she wants sex; 'make love' framed as an unwanted imposition
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through character integration and the precise inversion. The standard cultural narrative is that women want 'making love' and men want 'fucking.' Fleabag inverts this with complete specificity to her psychological profile (sex as performance and avoidance, established at 05:53). 'All he wants to do is make love' treats emotional intimacy as an imposition — the 'all he wants to do' framing makes it sound like a complaint you'd make about a partner who won't stop vacuuming. Rewatch bonus: it deepens given what we learn about her grief and dissociation.

jokes.text: I wish he'd just fuck me. All he wants to do is make love.
jokes.setup: Harry and Fleabag are reuniting after the break-up — a tender moment. 'Are you OK?' 'Yeah, I'm really good. I'm amazing.'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's aside to camera: 'I wish he'd just fuck me. All he wants to do is make love.' — the distinction between fucking and making love delivered as a genuine complaint.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. The specific complaint — not that the sex is bad but that it's too loving — reveals Fleabag's core problem: intimacy, not sex. 'All he wants to do is make love' as a complaint rephrases devotion as a burden. This could only be Fleabag. Connects to the earlier 'not so much the feeling of it' observation and extends that portrait. 'He's wasting me' immediately after is also excellent — she believes love is a misuse of her potential.

jokes.text: He's wasting me. I was once fucking this guy who would breathe on every thrust... / You're so young! / I masturbate about that all the time.
jokes.setup: Fleabag, in the middle of sex, is confiding her sexual preferences to the audience while Harry is right there
jokes.punchline: Harry apparently says 'you're so young!' — seemingly responding to something she's doing or said, or as encouragement — and she immediately notes she masturbates about a different man. The compartmentalization is total
jokes.explanation: The mid-sex camera aside is peak Fleabag structure. 'I was once fucking this guy who would breathe on every thrust' as a half-completed thought — the specific 'breathe on every thrust' is vivid enough to generate a reaction before Harry's 'you're so young!' interrupts. 'I masturbate about that all the time' is delivered immediately after as if it's a normal aside.

jokes.text: I masturbate a lot these days. Especially when I'm bored. Or angry. Or upset. [...] Or happy.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is confiding her masturbation habits while in the middle of having sex with Harry
jokes.punchline: The list covers every emotional state — bored, angry, upset — and the pause before the addition of 'or happy' reveals she's covered everything. Masturbation as a response to all emotional weather
jokes.explanation: The structure of the list is well-engineered: the obvious candidates (bored, angry, upset) make the addition of 'or happy' work as a topper because it closes off any remaining non-masturbation states. Character-specific: given what we know about Fleabag's use of sex/masturbation as emotional avoidance (from 05:53), this deepens rather than just shocks. The timing — during sex — makes the dissociation visible.

jokes.text: Look, I think we should stop masturbating. And don't say anything yet...
jokes.setup: After sex, apparently, Fleabag makes a proposal to Harry
jokes.punchline: 'I think we should stop masturbating' as the post-sex declaration — the gravity of the opening 'Look' paired with this specific subject. The 'and don't say anything yet' implies she knows it's a lot to process
jokes.explanation: 'Look' as the opening signals a Serious Relationship Talk, making the subject ('we should stop masturbating') funnier. The 'and don't say anything yet' elevates this — she's anticipated his reaction, she wants him to hear her out, she has a full plan around not masturbating.

jokes.text: I've hidden our vibrators. / Our?
jokes.setup: Fleabag continues her masturbation-cessation plan by revealing she's hidden the vibrators
jokes.punchline: Harry's 'Our?' — gently pointing out that vibrators are not typically shared property in the way 'our' implies
jokes.explanation: The best one-word punchline of the episode — 'Our?' is perfect. It questions the possessive without any further comment. Harry's gentle bafflement at 'our vibrators' as a unit of property is funny because he's clearly trying to be a good sport while also wondering whose vibrators these are exactly. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: Well, I think you're being really sexy. / Don't! / Oh! I'm joking. I never masturbate. I don't know how.
jokes.setup: Harry says he thinks the no-masturbating plan is sexy. Fleabag immediately shuts this down
jokes.punchline: 'I never masturbate. I don't know how.' — delivered presumably with complete sincerity as a cover story, which given we've just heard Fleabag's masturbation confession monologue is spectacularly untrue
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ as a callback with perfect ironic structure. The audience has just heard Fleabag's extensive masturbation confession — she masturbates when bored, angry, upset, happy — and Harry now claims he never masturbates and doesn't know how, presumably to make Fleabag feel less embarrassed about the proposal. But it's also possible Harry is entirely sincere and genuinely doesn't, which adds another layer. 'I don't know how' is the topper — not just 'I don't' but 'I lack the technical knowledge.'

jokes.text: Also, I thought we should try and surprise each other once every day... / Are you getting this out of a book?
jokes.setup: Fleabag presents her relationship renovation plan — daily surprises
jokes.punchline: Harry's immediate 'Are you getting this out of a book?' — the gentle diagnosis that this is self-help literature rather than genuine desire
jokes.explanation: Solid character observation — Harry sees through the self-help rhetoric. 'Are you getting this out of a book?' is a well-observed line about how relationship management often sounds like it's been copy-pasted from a wellness guide.

jokes.text: How are you? / Quiet day? / Yeah, I'm fine. / You OK? You look stressed. / Well, I'm successful, so...
jokes.setup: Claire unexpectedly visits the cafe. Normal pleasantries are exchanged. Fleabag says she looks stressed
jokes.punchline: 'Well, I'm successful, so...' — Claire treats success and stress as synonymous, or as an explanation for stress. The ellipsis trails off into the implication that being stressed is the price of success and she accepts this entirely
jokes.explanation: 'Well, I'm successful, so...' is highly quotable and deeply Claire-specific. The logic is: success = stress, therefore stress = success, therefore being told she looks stressed is actually a compliment she's accepting. The trailing 'so...' implies the rest of the sentence is too obvious to complete. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Do you have rye bread? / No, but I have some normal bread you can puke up after.
jokes.setup: Claire asks for rye bread — a status food choice that implies health consciousness
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's response implies Claire has an eating disorder — or at least disordered eating habits. The leap from 'no rye bread' to 'bread you can puke up' is both brutal and delivered with complete cheerfulness
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through economy and the blunt specificity. 'Some normal bread you can puke up after' is casually devastating — it implies intimate knowledge of Claire's issues and a complete lack of filter or sympathy. Claire's response ('Great') is the straight-man beat that makes it worse. The joke is in the absolute cheerfulness of the delivery — Fleabag has no malice, she's just stating what she observes.

jokes.text: Great.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just suggested Claire use the normal bread to purge with.
jokes.punchline: Claire says 'Great' — not offended, apparently treating this as a reasonable suggestion. Her compliance is the darkest part.
jokes.explanation: One word that does enormous work. Claire saying 'Great' to the eating disorder joke makes Fleabag's dark implication land as character revelation — either Claire finds it funny, or she's not insulted because it's true, or she just automatically accepts anything as fine. Any of these interpretations is funnier and darker than the joke itself. Perfect economy: one word completes the joke.

jokes.text: Claire organises her own surprise party — 'I can organise it, do the food, act surprised, and just take it off your hands.' — completing the full collapse of the surprise party fiction
jokes.setup: The surprise party was invented as a cover story by Claire, then Fleabag was trapped organising it; now Claire is here to take it back
jokes.punchline: Claire's full self-sufficiency speech — she will organise it, do the food, AND act surprised — reveals the absurdity of surprise parties as a concept entirely. The party has no purpose except as a fiction that both parties are now maintaining
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ as a sustained structural joke. The escalation from cover story → Fleabag trapped → Claire taking it back and promising to 'act surprised' at a party she's now organising herself is a perfect comic loop. The idea of a woman organising her own surprise party and then acting surprised is both a specific joke about Claire's need for control and a general joke about the theatre of social rituals. 'I mean, I've done it, it's done. It's this Friday at 7:30 at mine.' delivered with brisk efficiency is the funniest moment in the sequence.

jokes.text: How behind are you? If it's money that you need... / I don't need money. / That'll be £25, please.
jokes.setup: Claire offers financial help after asking how behind Fleabag is. Fleabag refuses
jokes.punchline: Immediately charges Claire £25 for the tomato sandwich — definitively proving she does need money while maintaining the fiction that she doesn't
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through the structural perfection and character integration. 'I don't need money' followed immediately by '£25 please' for a tomato sandwich is both a joke about her financial desperation and a character moment: Fleabag won't accept charity but will absolutely overcharge her sister. The £25 price tag is also a callback to the earlier £12.55 sandwich joke, escalating the extortion. The juxtaposition of 'I don't need money' and the price tag is timed perfectly.

jokes.text: Can't believe that thing's still alive.
jokes.setup: The guinea pig (Hillary) squeaks. Claire refers to it
jokes.punchline: 'Can't believe that thing's still alive' — delivered with the flat contempt of someone who expected/hoped for a different outcome
jokes.explanation: Small dark joke — 'that thing' depersonalises the guinea pig that Boo named and took seriously; Claire's amazement at its continued survival implies she'd prefer it otherwise. Modest but effective.

jokes.text: Oh, any news on Harry? / Yeah, we're back together. / Oh, God, I can't keep up.
jokes.setup: Claire asks about Harry as a conversational follow-up
jokes.punchline: They're back together — already — and Claire's 'Oh, God, I can't keep up' is the sister's exhaustion with the on/off relationship
jokes.explanation: 'I can't keep up' is Claire's first genuinely warm/relatable moment in the episode, which makes it funnier. It also functions as a meta-comment on the episode's Harry will-they/won't-they structure.

jokes.text: Harry? [looks into her flat, finds someone else there] Oh. Shit.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has returned home, apparently now back with Harry per what she just told Claire. She calls his name
jokes.punchline: Someone unexpected is in the flat — presumably the man from the shop (Jake). The 'Oh. Shit.' as reaction is the comedy of consequences
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the timing — she just told Claire they were back together, and now Jake is in her flat. The 'Surprise, surprise, surprise, surprise' that follows (presumably from Jake) confirms this. Moderate impact.

jokes.text: Surprise! [jumping out at Fleabag in shower] / Why would you do that? I thought I was going to get raped!
jokes.setup: Jake has taken the 'daily surprise' plan seriously — he's hidden somewhere and jumped out at Fleabag
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's response — 'I thought I was going to get raped!' — is the darkly honest response to being jumped at in one's home, which is not the direction the 'surprise' game was supposed to go
jokes.explanation: The callback to Fleabag's relationship renovation plan working too literally is solid. 'I thought I was going to get raped' is the kind of dark honest response the show is known for — the thing you actually think in that situation stated without filter.

jokes.text: It was a good joke.
jokes.setup: Harry apologizes, saying 'I'm sorry, it was a joke.' The natural response would be 'It's OK' or anger.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's 'It was a good joke' — conceding the joke's quality mid-genuine-trauma, as if she's a comedy critic evaluating the scare she just had.
jokes.explanation: A five-word line that is completely unique to Fleabag's character. Even when genuinely scared, she can't help being a comedy critic. The acknowledgment that it was a good joke while still shaking demonstrates her inability to not evaluate humor — which is itself characterful and slightly tragic. Very Fleabag.

jokes.text: It was a good joke. [Harry says this after Fleabag nearly had a heart attack from the surprise]
jokes.setup: Harry is still recovering from the terror of the surprise. He apologises.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag — the one who was nearly frightened to death — validates the joke with 'It was a good joke.' This is both generous and slightly deranged
jokes.explanation: A small grace note — Fleabag acknowledging the joke's quality even while still in shock. Characterizes her relationship with comedy as something she respects even when it's at her expense.

jokes.text: That was horrible. / It was a surprise. / I know. / Thank you.
jokes.setup: They're processing the aftermath of the surprise. Fleabag says it was horrible
jokes.punchline: 'Thank you' — after 'I know' (confirming it was indeed horrible), Fleabag thanks him anyway, because she asked for a surprise
jokes.explanation: 'Thank you' as the end of that sequence is a small perfect beat — she's simultaneously confirming it was awful and honouring the spirit of the request. The exchange has a rhythm: That was horrible / It was a surprise / I know / Thank you — each line is doing character work in about two or three words.

jokes.text: I know what you look like! I know what you took! That is the last time!
jokes.setup: Martin is apparently on the phone, in a rage
jokes.punchline: This is the consequence of Fleabag's threats and the sculpture theft — Martin threatening her, but the phrasing 'I know what you look like' is funny as a threat from a family member you see regularly
jokes.explanation: 'I know what you look like' as a threat to your brother-in-law is comedy of the absurd — of course he knows what she looks like. The impotent rage of Martin's threats is consistent with his character.

jokes.text: I had to go into the history on my computer to find something I'd seen on the H&M website this morning and... I don't want to point fingers but... anal, gang bang, mature, big cock, small tits, hentai, Asian, teen, MILF, big butts, lesbian, gay, facial, fetish, bukake, young and old, swallow, rough, voyeur and public.
jokes.setup: Fleabag prefaces with an extremely relatable and domestic setup: she needed to find something on H&M she'd looked at earlier
jokes.punchline: The list of search terms — an exhaustive, eclectic, non-discriminating catalogue of every available category — read in sequence as if a shopping list
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's peak joke. Justified 9+ across all dimensions. The setup is one of the best in the episode: 'I don't want to point fingers but...' is an absolute masterclass of a bridge between 'I was looking for an H&M dress' and the list. The list itself is funny on multiple levels: (1) its sheer length and variety — Martin watches everything, he has no genre loyalty whatsoever; (2) the alphabetic/categorical disorder of it, suggesting genuine browser history rather than curated selection; (3) the inclusion of 'lesbian, gay' alongside the heterosexual content (he's comprehensive); (4) 'bukake' appearing casually mid-list; (5) the resolution with 'and public' as the final entry. This is also the payoff to the callback established at 02:12 — Fleabag predicted 'gang bangs, Asian' and got confirmation plus an entire catalogue. The 'H&M' domestic framing makes the contrast with the content devastating. The list is so good it approaches the 'snip snap snip snap' calibration anchor.

jokes.text: Why are you being so sexy? / Don't make me hate you. Loving you's painful enough.
jokes.setup: Harry has apparently been crying and delivered what Fleabag considers an extremely powerful line
jokes.punchline: The line itself — 'Don't make me hate you. Loving you's painful enough' — is genuinely beautiful and moving. The comedy is that Harry has inadvertently written good lyrics while having a breakdown
jokes.explanation: The comedy is meta — Harry says something genuinely poetic in the middle of emotional devastation, and Fleabag's response is to note how good it is as song material. 'Don't make me hate you. Loving you's painful enough' is actually a very good line. The joke is that Fleabag's instinct is to appreciate the craft rather than the feeling.

jokes.text: OK, sorry, but I really think you should write that down... For your songs and stuff. It's perfect! It's poetic yet real. Serious.
jokes.setup: Harry has just delivered an emotionally devastating breakup speech
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's response is to suggest he transcribe it for his songwriting — turning his real pain into content feedback
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific: Fleabag's immediate pivot from emotional content to craft assessment is consistent with her coping mechanism of intellectualizing and aestheticizing pain. 'Poetic yet real' as a literary critique of your own breakup is both funny and revealing. 'Serious' as the final word — as if Harry needs to be convinced she means it as a compliment — is perfect.

jokes.text: Don't... make me... / Hate you. / Yeah, I know, thank you.
jokes.setup: Harry reprises his own line for her
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's 'Yeah, I know, thank you' — completing the line as if confirming its quality, while also being dismissive
jokes.explanation: A solid small callback. Fleabag's 'Yeah, I know, thank you' is both completion and dismissal — she's already memorized it for him, she approves, she'd like to move on.

jokes.text: He's going to write that down. [watching Harry]
jokes.setup: Harry has delivered his 'don't make me hate you' speech and is now leaving
jokes.punchline: Fleabag observes that he's going to write it down — her prediction that he'll instrumentalize his own pain the same way she asked him to. He is doing exactly what she suggested
jokes.explanation: A neat small payoff — Harry is taking her notes literally while also having a genuine breakdown. The comedy of watching someone process their own pain as potential content.

jokes.text: I'm not going to clean. / It's still in pretty good shape, so...
jokes.setup: Harry is doing his breakup packing. He makes a declaration
jokes.punchline: 'It's still in pretty good shape, so...' — Harry acknowledges the last cleaning he did (presumably during a previous break-up) is still holding. The flat's cleanliness is a passive record of their break-up history
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ as a callback that perfectly pays off the earlier cleaning jokes. 'It's still in pretty good shape, so...' implies Harry cleans so thoroughly that his work lasts across multiple cycles of the relationship. The trailing 'so...' is the comedy — what does he mean? He's declining to clean because his previous clean is still valid? This is a man who maintains a cleaning legacy across breakups. Perfectly circular, deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: DOOR SLAMS / DOOR OPENS / Forever. / DOOR SLAMS
jokes.setup: Harry has said goodbye dramatically. The door slams. Then reopens immediately
jokes.punchline: 'Forever.' — Harry has come back to add 'forever' to his goodbye, making it more permanent, then leaves again. The addition of 'forever' required a full re-entry
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through economy and callback precision. One word — 'Forever' — delivered between two door slams, requiring Harry to re-enter specifically to upgrade his goodbye. It's both absurd (the timing, the effort, the single word) and character-perfect (Harry commits to his break-ups). The rhythm of SLAM / OPEN / 'Forever.' / SLAM is pure physical comedy timing even in transcript form. Highly quotable as a single word in context.

jokes.text: He'll be back.
jokes.setup: Harry has dramatically declared 'Forever' and slammed the door. It's a devastating final exit
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's instant 'He'll be back' — deflating every dramatic gesture Harry has made throughout the episode with two words
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ as an episode-closing callback. 'He'll be back' is the final word on everything — Harry's 'forever', his crime-scene cleaning, his daily surprises, his 'Don't make me hate you' song material, his door-slam re-entry to say 'forever.' All of that performance, and Fleabag knows it means nothing. The line mirrors 'It will' from the episode's opening minutes, completing a structural arc: in the opening she said the manipulation would work; in the closing she says the breakup won't last. Both statements delivered with identical flat certainty. The episode ends on her omniscient resignation, which is both funny and sad. Two words, maximum impact.

tmdb_overview: Haunted by memories of her dead best friend, dumped by her emotionally-fragile boyfriend, and now desperately trying to sell her stolen goods, Fleabag attempts to rekindle romantic fires to distract her from the mayhem of her life.
